title: Change
subtitle: {key:c} is delete-then-insert, atomically.
summary: {key:c} is the change operator. {key:c}{key:w} changes a word; {key:c}{key:c} changes a line; {key:C} changes to end of line. {key:S} and {key:s} are short forms that change a whole line and a single character respectively.

blocks.text: {key:c} is the shortcut for {key:d}-then-{key:i}: it deletes the same range {key:d} would, *and* drops you straight into *insert mode* at that spot. Then {key:Esc} closes the change as one undoable, dot-repeatable unit.

blocks.label: Change to end of line (same as {key:c}{key:$})

blocks.title: Change Is Delete + Insert as One Atomic "Change"
blocks.text:
Internally, {key:c}{motion} does three things:

  1. Cuts the range covered by {motion} into the unnamed and numbered registers (just like {key:d}).
  2. Switches to *insert mode* at the start of the cut range.
  3. Marks an undo boundary that closes when you press {key:Esc}.

Step 3 is the important one. The whole episode — delete, type, leave *insert mode* — becomes **one** entry in the undo tree. {key:u} reverts all of it. {key:.} replays all of it.

That's why changing a word, pressing {key:Esc}, moving to the next match with {key:n}, and pressing {key:.} is one of the most useful patterns in Vim: jump to next match, change the word, repeat. Each repeat is one undoable operation, not many. See [*Change-Next-Match Loop — {key:cgn}*](#patterns.cgn-dot) for the polished form.

Watch out: {key:cw} *behaves like* {key:ce} (change to end of word) — not what {key:dw} does. This is a deliberate, ancient quirk. The change operator with a word motion changes *just the word*, not the trailing whitespace. Most people prefer it that way.

Three handy shortcuts to remember: {key:C} is the same as {key:c}{key:$} (change to end of line). {key:S} is the same as {key:c}{key:c} (change the whole line). {key:s} is the same as {key:c}{key:l} (substitute one character). {key:s} doesn't get a lot of love — it's a one-character substitute, useful when you want to type a replacement immediately and skip the explicit {key:r}-then-look-around.

rows: Change inner word (text object; see *Text Objects*)
Change around quotes (text object; see *Text Objects*)

blocks.title: Change is delete-then-insert, with one historical quirk
blocks.text: {key:c} is not a primitive: it runs the delete operator, then enters *insert mode* at the landing position. The famous quirk that {key:c}{key:w} behaves like {key:c}{key:e} falls out of this — change adjusts the motion's endpoint to drop the trailing whitespace so the inserted text doesn't push it. Knowing change is a composition lets you predict its behavior with any motion you've already learned.
